data.title: Midday Need to Know: SpaceX makes historic debut, Copper rallies & more
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages are higher on Friday as traders gear up for the highly anticipated SpaceX (SPCX) initial public offering.

The blue chip Dow (DJI) was +0.9% the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was 0.5%, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.3%.

Now, here are four news stories that broke in the morning to watch out for:

SpaceX makes historic debut: SpaceX (SPCX) shares officially opened on the Nasdaq at $150, an 11% gain from the $135 IPO price, in what became the most valuable company ever to go public with an initial valuation of $1.75T. The offering raised $75B and was oversubscribed by a factor of four, with more than $350B in total orders placed. The debut made Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire, with his stake in SpaceX now worth roughly $866B.

Copper rallies on Iran deal hopes: Copper and copper miners are extending gains Friday, lifted by the prospect of an agreement to end the U.S.-Iran war. President Trump said a deal could be signed within days, and Iran's semi-official Mehr News Agency published a 14-point draft deal that includes a possible reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Benchmark copper futures traded up 1.5% to $13,690/ton on the London Metal Exchange. Freeport-McMoRan rose 1.6% pre-market after surging 6.8% Thursday, while Southern Copper gained 1.2% following an 8.6% jump in the prior session.

Woodside Energy surges on Exxon takeover speculation: Woodside Energy (WDS)  jumped 6.8% in Friday trading following a Bloomberg report that Exxon Mobil (XOM) is studying the Australian company as a potential acquisition target to expand its presence in liquefied natural gas and Asian markets. Woodside, Australia's largest LNG exporter, has secured long-term sales agreements with leading buyers across Asia, including South Korea and Japan. An LNG-focused deal has become a more serious priority since the Iran war effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz and prompted Asian buyers to seek alternative suppliers.

Nvidia hires new government affairs chief: Nvidia (NVDA) has hired lobbyist Bruce Andrews to run its government affairs office in Washington, D.C. Andrews formerly served as head of government affairs at Intel under former CEO Pat Gelsinger and was Deputy Secretary of the U.S. Commerce Department during the Obama Administration. He starts his new position on Monday, June 15.

data.title: Piper Sandler assumes coverage of five regional banks
data.description:
Piper Sandler assumed coverage of five regional banks, with Arrow Financial (AROW) rated Overweight, while Cass Information Systems (CASS), Fulton Financial (FULT), Medallion Financial (MFIN), and OceanFirst Financial (OCFC) rated Neutral.

Arrow Financial should continue its net interest margin rebound while adding franchise value from a greater commercial loan growth focus in its attractive upstate New York footprint, according to the investment bank.

The trajectory and a recent acquisition drive Piper Sandler's expectation for strong EPS growth.

On CASS, the investment bank said payments fee revenue growth has stagnated on a recession in freight, but dollar volumes are picking up on inflation. "We see synergies in terms of payment rails from the bank, but wonder if it serves as a cap to the valuation, which trends high for banks."

Fulton Financial's steady profitability improvement is fairly valued, asset sensitivity has stepped down, and the company is targeting more M&A.

Meanwhile, Medallion Financial's strong loan growth may take time to build to stronger earnings, steps to lower consumer credit losses could lead to solid risk-adjusted returns, while proxy fights remind of a weaker financial past.

Piper Sandler rated OCFC as Neutral on middling profitability as the company digests the FFIC acquisition.

data.title: Space and satellite stocks tumble after SpaceX IPO
data.description:
A long list of space-related and satellite stocks swung lower on Friday after SpaceX's (SPCX) IPO fired off at a trading level below many of the high-flying expectations of investors.

Notable movers included Virgin Galactic (SPCE) -30%, Rocket Lab (RKLB)  -8%, York Space Systems (YSS) -17%, Planet Labs (PL) -10%, Firefly Aerospace (FLY) -18%, Redwire (RDW) -8%, Satellogic (SATL) -11%, EchoStar (SATS) -12%, AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) -13%, Sidus Space (SIDU) -14%,  BlackSky Technologies (BKSY) -11%, and Intuitive Machines (LUNR) -11%.

Many of those names were bid up over the last week on the expectation of a giant rush of investment into the space-related stocks following the SpaceX (SPACE) IPO. Most of the stocks are off their session lows in the last 20 minutes.

data.title: SEALSQ sets first QSOC satellite launch in Q4, but shares are still in red zone
data.description:
SEALSQ (NASDAQ: LAES) announced on Friday that it plans to launch its first Quantum Security Operations Center (QSOC) satellite on a SpaceX mission in Q4 2026, with plans to expand the network to as many as 100 satellites by 2033.

Built with support from WISeKey’s subsidiaries, the company said the launch is the first step toward building a network of satellites that will provide post-quantum cybersecurity, AI services, and secure communications from space.

The initiative comes as demand for quantum-resistant cybersecurity solutions continues to grow amid rising AI adoption and concerns over future cyber threats.

Founder and CEO Carlos Moreira said the initiative builds on technologies validated through 21 SpaceX satellite missions since 2023.

Despite the long-term opportunity, SEALSQ shares have fallen ~3.83% to ~$3.08 today. Additionally, the stock is down 16.8% YTD versus an 8.02% gain of the S&P 500 (SP500) returns over the same period. 

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings system gives the stock a Strong Sell rating with a score of 1.30. Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha analysts rate the stock a Buy with a score of 4.00, while Wall Street analysts have a Strong Buy rating of 4.50.

data.title: Exxon weighing takeover targets including Woodside Energy - Bloomberg
data.description:
Woodside Energy (WDS) up 6.8% in Friday's trading following a Bloomberg report that Exxon Mobil (XOM) is studying the Australian company as a potential acquisition target among options to expand its presence in liquefied natural gas and Asian markets.

Exxon (XOM) has been holding early-stage discussions internally, and Woodside (WDS) is one of several targets the company has been evaluating, according to the report.

Woodside (WDS), Australia's largest LNG exporter, has secured long-term sales agreements with leading buyers across Asia, including in South Korea and Japan, making it an attractive target for companies seeking a foothold in the LNG market.

An LNG-focused deal has become a more serious priority since the start of the Iran war, which effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz and prompted buyers in Asia to seek alternative suppliers outside the Middle East.

Woodside (WDS) and Exxon (XOM) are already partners in the Bass Strait project, with Woodside taking over the operatorship last year.

data.title: Citizens downgrades Broadstone Net Lease, upgrades EPR Properties
data.description:
EPR Properties (EPR) was upgraded at Citizens, while Broadstone Net Lease (BNL) was downgraded as the financial services firm updated its net-lease REIT coverage.

For EPR Properties, Citizens sees "opportunity for a meaningful pickup in deployment, portfolio operating trends have remained resilient, while the movie industry appears poised for a rebound throughout the remainder of 2026, which supports the potential for improved rent coverage."

The stock was upgraded to Market Outperform from Market Perform, with the price target set at $70.

The rating aligns with the Seeking Alpha authors rating of Buy. Wall Street analysts grade the stock as Hold, while the Quant Rating system rates it as Strong Buy.

Meanwhile, Broadstone Net Lease's shares are trading relatively in line with Citizens' prior $21 price target, coupled with leverage trending well-ahead of the sector average, with an expanding development pipeline that is expected to keep this metric elevated for the near term.

BNL was cut to Market Perform from Market Outperform, in line with the SA authors rating of Hold. Sell-side analysts grade the stock as Buy, while Quant sees it as Strong Buy.

data.title: Nvidia hires former Obama official to head its government affairs office
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) has hired lobbyist Bruce Andrews to run its government affairs office in Washington, D.C.

Andrews formerly served as the head of government affairs at Intel (INTC) under former CEO Pat Gelsinger. He was also the Deputy Secretary of the U.S. Commerce Department during the Obama Administration.

"I am super excited to join the incredible NVIDIA team," Andrews said in a post on LinkedIn on Thursday. "NVIDIA is a remarkable company at the center of the AI revolution that is transforming the world's largest industries and profoundly impacting society. I'm looking forward to helping NVIDIA lead the AI revolution and reach new breakthroughs for America and the world."

Andrews left his position at Covington & Burling, an elite law firm, to work for the Jensen Huang-led tech titan. He starts his new position on Monday, June 15.

"It has been a great privilege to be a part of the world-class Covington team and to have the opportunity to work alongside such exceptional, collaborative and deeply collegial professionals," Andrews said.

data.title: Xbox CEO signals 'hardware component crisis,' prompting business model reset: Wedbush
data.description:
Microsoft's (MSFT) Xbox CEO Asha Sharma released an internal memo this week that indicated the division is facing a "hardware component crisis" causing console storage components to skyrocket.

Sharma said storage component costs had doubled by last fall and have doubled again since then. They are expected to increase fivefold by the 2027 holiday season compared to two years earlier. Memory is on a similar trajectory.

Wedbush analysts contend that the memory and NAND supply crunch, which is being driven by the AI data center buildout, is forcing consumer electronics companies to create entirely new business models. PC maker Lenovo is also expected to increase prices for its products in July.

"We view the Xbox memo as a clear sign that the memory/NAND shortage is rewriting consumer hardware economics (not just trimming margins)," said Wedbush analysts Matt Bryson and Antoince Legault in an investor note. "Client SSD NAND that ran 5-6 cents/GB as recently as 2H 2025 is now closer to $0.30/GB, which on our estimate adds $250 of storage cost to a 1TB Series X (a $650 console) and $500 to a 2TB Special Edition ($800). That a platform holder is publicly weighing a new hardware model and flagging constrained console output shows the pass through has moved from PCs and phones into fixed BOM devices that can't easily reprice, reinforcing our structural shortage through 2027+ thesis."

The memo revealed that the Xbox division is planning for layoffs at the end of the company's fiscal year on June 30 as part of a sweeping reorganization of the business. The division also plans to "significantly slash" budgets for marketing and other areas amid a "resetting of the business."

Sharma was appointed CEO of Xbox in February.

data.title: UDR sees operational growth ahead; strengthens investor outreach
data.description:
UDR (UDR) is optimistic about its outlook as new apartment supply in the Sunbelt moderates, and it sticks to its knitting while two major rivals are set to merge. Furthermore, the REIT has been intensifying its outreach to investors.

The company will switch to paying a monthly dividend from quarterly, starting in July, to bolster its profile among prospective investors.

“We think that we can attract some new investors to our story, and that, in turn, should help lift the stock price and benefit all investors,” Chief Financial Officer Dave Bragg said in an interview with Seeking Alpha.

There’s a “minor administrative cost” to increasing the frequency of the dividend, but he sees the higher profile as more than offsetting the costs. It also expands its access to capital, one of the three main points the apartment REIT is making to investors. The other two are operational excellence, which involves maximizing revenue growth and margins, and capital allocation.

In capital allocation, the company sees a limited arbitrage opportunity. “If our stock trades at a meaningful discount to the underlying value of assets — which has been the case for all of the apartment REITs — it allows us to temporarily sell assets and buy back stock and create value,” Bragg said. Last month, the company increased its share repurchase authorization by 25M shares.

That’s also improving its asset base, setting it up for improved growth, because it’s divesting the properties that are deemed as inferior performers.

Once the stock revives, there’s another opportunity. “If you see the stock trade above the underlying value of the assets, that could be an opportunity to issue equity and grow,” he said.

“But to run the business on a day-to-day basis, we have more than enough capital due to what we generate,” Bragg explained. “It helps us fund the capital expenditure needs for the portfolio, and currently the playbook that we've been reading from is mostly centered around selling assets and buying back stock.”

“Everything that we do, we want it to benefit our existing shareholders,” he said, pointing to UDR’s (UDR) dividend yield within the 4.5%-5% range and its track record of paying out $9B of dividends over its 50-year history.

With the year almost half over, UDR (UDR) is continuing to perform in line with expectations, Bragg said. At the beginning of the year, the REIT suggested guidance for blended rent growth of 1.5%-2%. Q1 results were in that range, and Q2 to date is also performing within that expectation, he said.

Meanwhile, occupancy remains elevated in the 96.5 to 97% range, which is the highest among apartment REITs. “And we're controlling expenses,” he added. “So we think that this year, through the first five months, is on track with our expectations.”

As has been the case recently, coastal markets continue to be stronger than Sunbelt markets. San Francisco and New York City are UDR’s (UDR) strongest markets currently, with Philadelphia and Orange County, California, improving. 

The Sunbelt, which has suffered from oversupply in recent years, “is still searching for an inflection point that will allow for a meaningful acceleration of rent growth,” he said. Bragg expects that to occur when job growth picks up. With the amount of new supply now declining, the region is “poised for a nice recovery.”

The company’s tenant retention rate has improved as UDR (UDR) uses a data-driven assessment of tenant satisfaction. “As a result, our turnover has dropped more than 900 basis points since 2023,” he said. The percentage of residents that leave every year has declined to under 40%, compared with the 55%-60% level when Bragg started in the apartment REIT business. “It suggests to us that residents really enjoy living at UDR communities, and they're satisfied, and they're willing to stay longer and pay more to do so,” he said.

Asked whether the proposed merger of Equity Residential (EQR) and AvalonBay Communities (AVB) will change the landscape for UDR (UDR), Bragg said, “It does not. We are focused on what we can control, which is excellence in all facets of the game.”

He continued, “These are two good, established companies with long track records of success. We look forward to seeing how it plays out, but it doesn't change anything for us.”

Apartment REIT stocks, overall, have declined in the past five years as a wave of new supply entered the market. UDR (UDR) stock fell 21% in five years, compared with the S&P 500's 76% advance. That may be turning around. Year to date, UDR stock has increased 7.4%, nearing the S&P 500's 8.9% improvement during the same period.

In Friday midday trading, UDR gained 0.8%.

data.title: SpaceX jumps 11% in historic IPO as Elon Musk becomes world's first trillionaire
data.description:
We have liftoff.

SpaceX's (SPCX) long-awaited initial public offering happened on Friday, as shares officially opened on the Nasdaq at $150, for a gain of 11%. 

Musk and AI

The offering makes Elon Musk, who leads both SpaceX and Tesla (TSLA), the world's first trillionaire.

That his net worth crossed the trillion-dollar mark after the debut is notable, given that much of his wealth is tied to the rocket and artificial intelligence company. His stake in SpaceX is worth roughly $866B, and he retained overwhelming voting control after the IPO, giving him effective authority over SpaceX's strategic direction and board composition.

According to a new filing, Musk holds options to purchase 350M Class B common shares at an exercise price of $8.3998 per share through Feb. 11, 2031. The Elon Musk Revocable Trust indirectly owns 526.2M Class A shares. Another vehicle, EM 2024 GRAT-A, indirectly owns 7.4M Class A shares.

The offering was led by Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Bank of America, Citigroup, and JPMorgan Chase, alongside a broader syndicate of banks. 

Goldman Sachs President John Waldron appeared on Bloomberg on Friday and said the offering was indicative of investor demand for funding all things related to artificial intelligence.

Recently, SpaceX has focused more on artificial intelligence than just putting rockets into space. Musk has talked about putting data centers into space, using the company's rockets to get them there. The company has other AI-related assets, including its Grok chatbot and its Colossus data centers.

It has also increasingly positioned itself as an AI infrastructure provider, providing computing power to Anthropic (ANTHRO) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) in separate deals, aside from its traditional businesses in launch services and satellite communications. Goldman Sachs recently said it expects SpaceX's revenue related to AI to jump 100 times between now and 2030.

Google's parent company, Alphabet, is a long-time investor in SpaceX.

Unusual nature 

In an unusual move, SpaceX priced 555.5M shares at $135 apiece, instead of allowing the book runners to determine the price based on supply and demand.

Another sign of the unusual nature of the offering is the heavy allocation for retail investors. Approximately 20% of the offering was granted to retail investors, a much higher percentage than most IPOs. Interest from retail investors reportedly topped $100B, including offers from outside the U.S.

Asset manager BlackRock (BLK) reportedly requested at least $5B worth of SpaceX shares, indicating heavy demand from institutional investors. In total, more than $350B in orders were placed, including $250B from institutional investors, Bloomberg reported.

For comparison purposes, artificial intelligence chipmaker Cerebras (CBS), which went public last month, increased its share price multiple times before the IPO, and only a few retail investors were granted an allocation, according to complaints on social media.

In total, SpaceX raised $75B in the offering, which was reportedly oversubscribed by a factor of four.

Another sign of the unusual nature of the offering was that several Wall Street investment firms have already started coverage on SpaceX. Earlier this week, Oppenheimer put an Outperform rating and a $190 price target on SpaceX, arguing the Elon Musk-led company is uniquely positioned to capitalize on the convergence of artificial intelligence, communications infrastructure, and space-based computing. 

Wedbush Securities said the offering is reflective of “an inflection” of space and artificial intelligence. “Overall, SpaceX going public is an important moment for the broader tech sector in our view as this AI Revolution and data takes this next step forward,” the research firm wrote in a note to clients.

Wedbush also maintained its position that the SpaceX offering will eventually lead to a merger with Tesla, noting that Tesla already owns a stake in SpaceX via its merger with xAI, which occurred earlier this year. 

“Musk wants to own and control more of the AI ecosystem and step by step the holy grail could be combining SpaceX and Tesla in some way to give the connected tissue between both disruptive tech stalwarts looking to lead the AI Revolution in this next tech chapter for the market,” analysts at the research firm wrote.

Changing the status quo

Adding on to the unusual nature of the IPO is that SpaceX will become eligible for inclusion in certain indexes, such as those provided by the Nasdaq (NDAQ) and FTSE Russell, much faster than previous offerings.

The speed of the inclusion has prompted some concern from investment officials from several large public pension systems in the U.S. They have asked the major index providers to justify the recent rule changes.

Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D.-MA) asked the Securities and Exchange Commission earlier this week to delay the offering. She argued the blockbuster offering posed unusual risks to retail investors, due to its valuation, governance structure, and expected inclusion in major stock indexes.

Despite the aforementioned concerns, many Americans already have some exposure to SpaceX, via ETFs owned by Cathie Wood’s Ark Invest or mutual funds owned by Fidelity Investment or Baron Funds. Venture capital firms Founders Fund and Andreessen Horowitz also own significant stakes in SpaceX.

Shattering records

With an initial valuation of $1.75T, SpaceX is the most valuable company ever to go public.

Alibaba (BABA) went public in September 2015 at a valuation of $169.4B, while Meta Platforms (META) (then known as Facebook) was valued at $81.3B in May 2012. Uber (UBER) was valued at $75.5B in May 2019. 

Only a handful of companies have ever even reached the rarified air of a valuation of $1T or more. Excluding Saudi Aramco, which does not trade in the U.S., the group is limited to names such as Nvidia (NVDA), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Tesla (TSLA), Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), and Walmart (WMT). Most of these names took decades to achieve such a financial feat.
